question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
the aim of the present study was
to summarize the available evidence to investigate whether
serum/plasma levels of Hcy were associated with SSc using
meta-analysis, and summarize recent literature data regarding
the reasons for the increased Hcy and the supposed
mechanisms involved in SSc.

Studies meeting the following
criteria were considered to be included in this meta-analysis:
(a) case-control study, clinical cohort or cross-sectional
study design; (b) human subjects with SSc; (c) detailed data
about plasma/serum level of Hcy in both patients and
healthy controls were accessible. If a study was reported in
more than one publication, the latest paper was considered
in our meta-analysis.

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?

to locate all relevant publications that documented the
association of serum levels of Hcy in SSc. Search terms
and key words were used as follows: Systemic sclerosis OR
scleroderma OR SSc individually with each of the

hyperhomocysteinemia, HHcy. Studies meeting the following
criteria were considered to be included in this meta-analysis:

about plasma/serum level of Hcy in both patients and

question: Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers:
after carefully reading the title and abstract, nine
articles [12-18,20,21] with abstracts in total were selected in
this systematic review (Table 1). However, in one study ,
only the median was reported. Finally, only eight eligible
studies including 475 cases and 265 controls were merged
by us.

I2 statistics and Q-test were used to
assess the degree of heterogeneity between studies. If p<.05
for the Q2 test or I2>50%, a random-effects model was
selected to pool the data. Otherwise, a fixed-effect model
was used.

We found significant between-study heterogeneity
and conducted analyses using random-effects models

question: Was between-study variation (heterogeneity) minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
No significant association was found between the serum levels
of Hcy and SSc (pooled SMD 1/41.382lmol/L,
95%CI 1/40.442 to 3.206, p  1/4.137; I2 1/498.7%, p 1/4.000), but t
there are two outlier studies [12,18] that deviate significantly
from most other studies, which made it difficult to generalize
these results. Therefore, we excluded two studies in sensitivity
analysis. After excluding these two studies, six
studies were included in the meta-analysis

First, significant heterogeneity was observed in this meta-analysis.
The heterogeneity of the population probably originates in
the differences in the characteristics of patients, such as age,
gender, region, study type and other residual confounding
effect. Indeed, the different measurement used to define Hcy
plasma level may also play a role in high heterogeneity.

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
In conclusion, this is the first preliminary meta-analysis
to assess the relationship of Hcy serum levels with SSc.
However, several limitations should be acknowledged. First,
significant heterogeneity was observed in this meta-analysis.
The heterogeneity of the population probably originates in
the differences in the characteristics of patients, such as age,
gender, region, study type and other residual confounding
effect. Indeed, the different measurement used to define Hcy
plasma level may also play a role in high heterogeneity.
Moreover, many factors have an effect on Hcy levels, such
as blood folate and vitamin B12 levels and polymorphisms
of genes involved in the metabolism of Hcy. Unfortunately,
due to the insufficient information of these factors, a comprehensive
meta-regression or subgroup analysis was not
successfully conducted. Thus, the results of meta-analysis
should be interpreted with caution. Therefore, it needs to be
demonstrated by well-designed prospective studies whether
Hcy was higher in SSc.
